coredata.dc:description: In the geological storage and utilization wells of CO2, the cement paste will undergo an early carbonization reaction with supercritical CO2 at the liquid-to-solid transition stage during the injection process. In the early stages of the hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction, liquid cement slurry exhibits high porosity and inadequate mechanical properties. In this work, the early carbonization reaction rate of the liquid cement paste is accelerated by adding graphene oxide (GO) to refine the pores of the cement and optimize the microstructure. The results show that GO can enhance the predominance of the hydration reaction in the cement paste during the synergistic process of early hydration and carbonation. This effect leads to an earlier appearance of the peak relative content of Ca(OH)2, a hydration product generated by the reaction, and shifts the peak time from 16 to 12 h. 29Si NMR spectra show that GO can increase the degree of polymerization of C-S-H, with the average chain length increasing from 5.79 to 6.53. SEM-EDS and μ-CT results show that in the early synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation (4–72 h), GO induced the orderly growth of CaCO3 and C-S-H seed crystals through the "carbon dot effect", forming a large number of flower-like structures, which reduced the porosity of cement from 2.15 % to 1.22 %.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the geological storage and utilization wells of CO2, the cement paste will undergo an early carbonization reaction with supercritical CO2 at the liquid-to-solid transition stage during the injection process. In the early stages of the hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction, liquid cement slurry exhibits high porosity and inadequate mechanical properties. In this work, the early carbonization reaction rate of the liquid cement paste is accelerated by adding graphene oxide (GO) to refine the pores of the cement and optimize the microstructure. The results show that GO can enhance the predominance of the hydration reaction in the cement paste during the synergistic process of early hydration and carbonation. This effect leads to an earlier appearance of the peak relative content of Ca(OH)2, a hydration product generated by the reaction, and shifts the peak time from 16 to 12 h. 29Si NMR spectra show that GO can increase the degree of polymerization of C-S-H, with the average chain length increasing from 5.79 to 6.53. SEM-EDS and μ-CT results show that in the early synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation (4–72 h), GO induced the orderly growth of CaCO3 and C-S-H seed crystals through the "carbon dot effect", forming a large number of flower-like structures, which reduced the porosity of cement from 2.15 % to 1.22 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The capture, utilization and geological storage of carbon dioxide (CO2) is the most promising technological approach to mitigate man-made global warming and prevent extreme climate change [1,2]. In the application of CCUS technology, Class-G oil well cement is often used as a wellbore sealing material or cement plug in the wellbore as an annular between the steel casing and the surrounding rock layer [3,4].

Class-G oil well cement produces alkaline Ca(OH)2 and C-S-H hydration products after hydration, which are susceptible to carbonation reactions with the CO2 bound in the wellbore. This carbonation reaction significantly degrades the mechanical properties, porosity, permeability and other properties of the cement paste and affects the lifetime of oil and gas wells [5,6]. Once the cement paste hardens, CO2 corrodes and damages the internal structure of the cement. In concrete, properties such as mechanical properties and impermeability are improved by using the early carbonization reaction (CO2 at ambient temperature and pressure) of the cement slurry [7–10]. During the cementing of CCUS wells, the liquid cement paste will also undergo an early carbonization reaction with the supercritical CO2 (ScCO2) in the wellbore. However, there are few studies on the mechanism of the early hydration-carbonization reaction and the evolution of the microstructure of the cement paste in the liquid-to-solid transition phase.

However, in the early carbonation of Portland cement under normal temperature and CO2 pressure, hydration and the formation of carbonation products are slow and the pore structure hardly changes, resulting in a weak protective effect for the cement paste [11]. In contrast, in CCUS wells, both the temperature and pressure of CO2 exceed its critical point (7.39 MPa and 31.1 °C), turning it into ScCO2. ScCO2 can easily penetrate and diffuse inside the cement due to its high diffusivity and low viscosity, affecting its internal structure and properties [12,13]. The high diffusivity of ScCO2 accelerates the early carbonation reaction of the cement paste, which leads to the formation of more CaCO3 crystals. The CaCO3 formed in this phase fills the pores of the cement paste, improves its pore structure and increases the subsequent mechanical properties of the cement [8,9]. In CCUS wells, the CO2 must be stored for long periods, often up to 50 years or longer. Therefore, it is important to improve the microstructure of the hydration and carbonation products of the cement by using structural optimizers. This approach aims to refine the pore space and improve the overall performance of the cement for long-term CO2 storage.

Graphene oxide (GO), a derivative of graphene, has transformed the use of nanomaterials in cement materials due to its exceptional mechanical and thermometric properties, good hydrophilicity, and numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface [14,15]. GO is known to significantly improve the mechanical properties of cement pastes with minimal additions [16–18] and can increase the density of cement [19,20]. In addition, GO can accelerate the cement hydration process by accelerating the nucleation growth and phase separation of the cement hydration products. The incorporation of GO has a significant influence on the hydration products (Ca(OH)2 and C-S-H) of cement and the alignment process, resulting in a uniform alignment of the typically irregular reaction products [21]. This template effect contributes to a more compact cement structure [16,22]. Overall, the versatile properties of GO indicate its potential as a material for optimizing the structure of hydration and carbonation products in cement, improving the performance of conventional silicate cement pastes and thus increasing the reliability of cement paste for applications in CCUS environments. However, there is no clear conclusion on how the template effect of graphene oxide (GO) affects the carbonation reaction of cementite hydration products during the early hydration of cement paste in the CCUS environment, and the structure and reaction process of carbonation products. Therefore, in this paper, the synergistic reaction rate and microstructural evolution mechanism of GO in the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction process of cement paste are analysed in depth concerning the problems in the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction process.

The physical phase and microstructural changes in the cement during the hydration-carbonation coupling process are characterized in this work using test methods such as infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetry (TG) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The content of the silicate gel in the hydration product and the change in chain length of the hydrated calcium silicate were analyzed by 29Si spectroscopic NMR. This study will support the mechanism of GO in the transition phase of solidification water-cement during the intrusion of ScCO2 in CCUS, providing an in-depth analysis of the synergistic reaction rate and microstructure evolution mechanism of GO in the early hydration-carbonation process of cement slurry. It also provides important theoretical guidance for the application of GO materials in the process of CCUS cementing cement.

The chemical composition and mass fraction of the G-grade oil well cement (Jiahua, Sichuan, China) used in the experiment are shown in
Tables 1 and 2. From the analysis of the FTIR test of GO in Fig. 1, it can be seen that the absorption peaks at 3455 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 for GO belong to the -OH stretching vibration and the C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively. C-O-C stretching vibration on the surface of the six-membered cyclic carbon lamellae, and the absorption peaks at 1633 cm−1 and 1105 cm−1 correspond to the -CO stretching vibration and -COOH stretching vibration at the edges of the six-membered cyclic carbon lamellae, respectively [23], indicating that the GO lamellae have been sufficiently oxidized by strong oxidants and strong acids and contain a higher number of oxygen-containing functional groups [24]. The SEM image of GO is shown in Fig. 2. It shows that GO is present in the form of lamellae and has many folds on the surface.

The cement paste formulations used for the experiments are shown in Table 3. The cement paste was prepared according to the cement paste formulation in Table 3 and the API RP 10B-2 standard [25,26]. Before preparing the cement paste, GO was first dispersed in water using an ultrasonic disperser for 120 min with a dispersion power of 500 w. In our early research, we found that increasing the dosage of GO to 0.10 wt%, The compressive strength of cement is higher [25]. So, the mass fraction of GO in the cement is determined to be 0.10 wt%. Then the cement paste was poured into the feeler tool (specimen size Φ25 × 25 mm) and numbered, and the sample preparation process is shown in Fig. 3. The density and flow properties of the prepared cement paste are shown in Table 3. Subsequently, the prepared cement samples were placed into the 90 °C, 8 MPa curing device (CO2 critical temperature of 31.26 °C and critical pressure of 7.29 MPa) as shown in Fig. 3. After curing to the target age (4, 8, 12, 16, 24 h and 3, 7, 14, 28 days), they were removed to terminate the hydration and tested accordingly.

After the cement paste had reached the predetermined reaction time, the cement specimen and quickly placed in liquid nitrogen for 30 minutes to freeze the slurry completely. Then some of the cement samples were removed and placed in a vacuum dryer pre-frozen to −60 °C, evacuated and continuously dried for 24 h [27]. After the termination of the hydration of the samples, the appropriate samples are prepared according to the test requirements.

The cement samples after the hydration-carbonization synergistic reaction were placed on the pressure testing machine platform (TYE-300B, China) with a loading rate of 71.7 kN/min ± 7.2 kN/min, and three parallel samples in each group were averaged.

XRD (DXJ-2000, Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to test the physical phase composition of cement paste after different ages of hydration-carbonation coupling. The experimental conditions were Cu-Kα radiation, 40 kV and 30 mA, 5–80° (0.04° step), and 11 min acquisition time for each diffractogram.

The contents of the cement hydration product (Ca(OH)2) and carbonization product CaCO3 were calculated by thermogravimetric analysis. The sample was heated on a thermal analyzer at 40–1000 °C (10 °C/min) with N2 shielding gas.

1 mg of the sample was weighed and 100 mg of KBr powder was thoroughly ground and pressed into tablets. The pressed tablets were placed on an FTIR spectrometer (Nikolais 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). Each measurement was averaged over 64 scans in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 (4 cm−1 step).

The porosity of the sample after the hydration-carbonization synergy reaction was analyzed using X-ray computed tomography (μ-CT) (Phoenix Vltomelx m, GE, USA). The samples were scanned at an X-ray energy of 240 kV/312 W with a resolution of 28.32 μm per voxel. 3D structural reconstruction of the pores and statistical analyses were carried out using VGStudio MAX 2023 (Volume Graphics, Germany) software [29].

The displacement of 29Si for the content of silica-oxygen tetrahedra in cement paste was tested using 29Si MAS NMR (Bruker Avance Neo 400WB, Germany). The experimental parameters were as follows: pulse duration of 4.0 μs, rotation rate of 10 kHz, and acquisition time of 20 ms.

The effect of GO on the micromorphology and elemental distribution of cement at the micro-morphology of the samples was analysed using SEM (ZEISS EVO MA15, Carl Zeiss, Germany). In addition, the elemental composition of the hydration and carbonation products was analysed using EDS (Oxford X-Max, UK). The upper and relatively flat lower surfaces of the selected samples were gold-sprayed to improve the electrical conductivity of the cement paste-hardened specimens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GO on compressive strength during cement hydration-carbonization synergistic reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 shows the changes in the compressive strength of cement in the GO group and the control group after different curing times in the supercritical CO2 environment. The compressive strength of cement is calculated as shown in Eq. (1). As shown in Fig. 4, the compressive strength of the cement in both the GO-doped and control groups increased with the extension of the reaction time, indicating that both the internal hydration products and carbonation products increased the compressive strength of the cement after the synergistic reaction of hydration and carbonation, which is consistent with the findings of researchers such as Liu [28,29]. The compressive strengths of the cement added with GO were all significantly higher than those of the control cement after the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction. This indicates that GO has a promoting effect on the hydration-carbonation coupling reaction of cement. The mechanism of its action is mainly that GO, a nanomaterial with a large specific surface area, can increase the contact area between cement particles and media such as H2O and CO2, thereby accelerating the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction of cement [30].

C: Compressive strength (MPa);
F: Indication of the pressure tester (kN);
r: Radius of cement specimen (mm).

According to Eq. (2), the growth rate of compressive strength of cement at different ages of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction is shown in Table 4. As can be seen from Table 4, the compressive strength of GO-cement at the early stage (1–3 days) of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction at 90 °C or 8 MPa CO2 increased by 2.90 MPa per day, which was higher than that of the control cement, which was 2.55 MPa, indicating that GO had a positive effect on the growth of the early strength of cement. The increase in the compressive strength of the cement did not exceed 1 MPa per day during the 3–14 days period of the synergistic reaction. In the late phase of the synergistic reaction (14–28 days), the growth rate of compressive strength did not exceed 0.4 MPa. This indicates that in the later stages of the synergistic reaction, the hydration reaction in the cement is more thorough and the strength development is slow. In addition, the compressive strength growth rate of GO cement was higher than that of the control cement at each stage of the synergistic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: μ-CT analysis of cement during synergistic hydration-carbonation reactions
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the reason for the increase in compressive strength of cement by GO, the experimental method of μ-CT was used in this section to perform non-destructive tomography on cement after 3 and 28 days of hydration-carbonation, and the pore structure was three-dimensionally reconstructed using VG Studio MAX 2023 software, and probability statistics on the pore volume and radius were performed. The changes in the pore volume of the cement after 3D reconstruction are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 5.

Fig. 5 shows the changes in the three-dimensional structure of the pores of control and GO cement after 3 and 28 days of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that in the early stage of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction (3 days), the internal pores of the control cement were larger, and the results of the quantitative analysis showed that the pore volume accounted for 2.15 % of the total cement volume (Table 5). On the other hand, the pore structure of the GO cement was significantly smaller than that of the control cement, and its pore volume accounted for only 1.22 % of the cement volume. When the reaction time was extended to 28 days, the total pore volume of the GO cement was 0.93 %, which was still significantly smaller than that of the control cement (1.88 %). From the results of the μ-CT scans of the cement, it can be seen that the incorporation of GO can refine the pores of the cement in the process of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction, making the cement denser and thus improving the compressive strength of the cement.

The variation in pore size and pore volume of cement was statistically analyzed using the VG Studio MAX 2023 software and the results are shown in Fig. 6. From Fig. 6, the pore radius and pore volume of the control cement were less than 0.8 mm and 0.8 mm3, respectively, when the coupled hydration-carbonation reaction was carried out up to 3 days, while the pores of the GO group were less than 0.5 mm and 0.4 mm3, respectively. This indicates that the cement can reduce the pore radius and pore volume in the early stage (3 days) of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction by incorporating GO. When the reaction lasted up to 28 days, the pore radius and pore volume of the control group were centrally distributed below 0.6 mm and 0.4 mm3, while the GO cement was distributed below 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm3. The pore volume and pore radius of the cement decreased compared to the reaction after 3 days. The main reason for this phenomenon is the continuation of the hydration reaction of the cement, which consumes the pore solution to form hydration and carbonation products to fill the pore space and make the pore structure of the cement denser. In addition, the pore radius and pore volume of the GO cement were still smaller than that of the control after 28 days of reaction.

For the cement pore volume and radius distribution in Fig. 6, the probability statistics of the pore volume distribution of cement were performed according to the volume sizes 0–0.2 mm3, 0.2–0.4 mm3, 0.4–0.6 mm3 and > 0.6 mm3, and the results are shown in Fig. 7. When the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction was carried out up to 3 days, the probability statistics of pore volume of cement in the control were 65.71 %, 22.62 %, 8.30 % and 3.77 %, respectively, while the probability statistics of the GO group were 98.40 %, 1.42 %, 0.11 % and 0.07 %, respectively. The probability statistics of the GO group were 32.69 % higher than those of the control group in the interval of 0–0.2 mm3 pore volume, while the statistics of the GO group were 11.9 % lower than those of the control group in the interval of pore volume > 0.4 mm3. Therefore, the incorporation of GO in the early stage of hydration-carbonation reaction (3 days) can significantly refine the pore volume and reduce the microporous structure of the cement.

In addition, the probability distributions of the pore volume of the cement in the control group after a reaction time of up to 28 days were 95.00 %, 3.79 %, 0.54 % and 0.66 %, while the statistical results in the GO group were 99.47 %, 0.38 %, 0.04 % and 0.11 %. The probability statistics of GO cement were 4.47 % higher than those of the control when the pore volume was in the interval of 0–0.2 mm3, while the probability statistics of the GO group were 4.18 % lower than those of the control group in the interval of pore volume > 0.4 mm3. In summary, the incorporation of GO can not only refine the pore structure in the early stage of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction (3 days), but also in the late stage of reaction (28 days), the pore structure of the cement is still denser than that of the control group, which is the main reason why the compressive strength of GO cement is still higher than that of the control group after 28 days of reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of products during the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction of cement
XRD analysis of cement during synergistic hydration-carbonation reactions
ce:section.ce:para: The compressive strength of the GO cement and the distribution of pore volume differed significantly from those of the control (3 days) due to the early stage of the synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation. The following analysis focuses on the changes in the microstructure and content of the products of the cement paste within 3 days (72 h) of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction to clarify the influencing mechanism of GO on the early synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction process and the development of the microstructure of the cement paste in the transition stage from liquid to solid.

Fig. 8 shows the XRD diffraction analysis profiles of control and GO cement after 4, 8, 12, 16, 24 and 72 h of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the diffraction peaks of Ca(OH)2 (PDF-# 44-1481), C3S (PDF-# 49-0442), C2S (PDF-# 83-0465) and the carbonation product calcite (PDF-# 88-2334) were more pronounced in the control sample, while the diffraction peak of aragonite (PDF-# 41-1475) appeared in the XRD pattern of GO cement (Fig. 8(b)) and other physical phases were consistent with the control cement. Due to the presence of CO2, distinct calcite diffraction peaks appeared in the XRD patterns of control and GO cement in the early stage of synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation (4 and 8 h), which were mainly generated by the carbonation of incompletely hydrated C3S and C2S directly with CO2 [31]. Moreover, the intensity of the Ca(OH)2 diffraction peaks of the internal hydration products of the control cement in the early stage of the synergistic reaction (4 and 8 h) was significantly lower than that of the GO group, while the intensity of the diffraction peaks of the incompletely hydrated C3S and C2S was greater than that of the GO group. This indicates that GO can accelerate the hydration process of cement to release more Ca(OH)2.

As shown in Fig. 8(b), clear aragonitic CaCO3 diffraction peaks appeared in the XRD pattern when the reaction time was extended up to 4 h, and the intensity of the diffraction peaks increased with the extension of the reaction time, which was significantly different from that of the control cement. The addition of GO formed not only calcite but also aragonite-type CaCO3 after the carbonation reaction between the hydration products of cement and CO2, and GO enriched the crystal types of CaCO3 formed during the carbonation process of cement paste.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TG/DTG analysis of cement during synergistic hydration-carbonation reactions
ce:section.ce:para:
The TG/DTG curves of the control and GO groups of cement at different ages of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction are shown in Figs. 9 and 10, respectively. As can be seen from Figs. 9 and 10, there are three decomposition peaks of the physical phase in cement at about 100, 450 and 720 °C, respectively. The conclusions of the corresponding studies showed that the weight loss ranges at 105–420 °C, 400–500 °C and 600–800 °C correspond to the decomposition of hydrate (C-S-H gel, Aft), Ca(OH)2 and CaCO3, respectively [32,33]. The relative contents of the hydration products Ca(OH)2 and CaCO3 were calculated according to Eqs. (3) and (4), and the results are shown in
Tables 6 and 7.

Note: It is calculated that for every 1000 g of oil well G-class cement clinker fully hydrated, 3.28 mol of Ca(OH)2 will be produced, with a mass fraction of 24.3 %.

As can be seen in Fig. 9, The DOH of the control cement increased from an initial 17.2 % (4 h) to 35.8 % (16 h) and then decreased to 18.9 % (72 h). This indicates that the hydration of C3S, C2S and other minerals in the control cement dominated for 16 h after the coupled hydration-carbonation reaction. Currently, the relative content of Ca(OH)2 in the cement peaks and the DOH of the cement reaches 35.8 %. As the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction continues, the hydration product Ca(OH)2 participates in the simultaneous carbonation reaction to form CaCO3, so the DOH of the cement paste decreases. The relative content of CaCO3 thus increases from 22.75 wt% to 38.84 wt%, which is also consistent with the abovementioned change in the intensity of the diffraction peaks of the physical XRD phases (Fig. 8).

The changes in the relative content of the individual physical phases in the GO cement are shown in Fig. 10 and Table 7. As can be seen from Fig. 10, with the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction, the DOH of cement paste in the GO group increased from 20.4 % (4 h) to 35.5 % (12 h) and then decreased to 19.8 % (72 h). This is consistent with the change rule of the relative content of cement hydration products in the control group. It is noteworthy that the peak of DOH of the GO group was at 12 h, 4 h earlier than that of the control cement. This indicates that the incorporation of GO accelerated the hydration reaction of cement. This phenomenon is caused by the fact that GO is a layered nanomaterial with a large specific surface area, which can increase the contact area between water and the cement mineral particles, thereby accelerating the early hydration reaction of cement, which is consistent with the above experimental XRD results.

Due to the fast hydration reaction rate of GO cement, the relative content of the carbonation product CaCO3 was higher in the early stages of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction (4–16 h) than in the control group. The CaCO3 crystals produced by the carbonation reaction fill the pores of the cement and make the pores of the GO cement denser than those of the control group. This is consistent with the above-mentioned analysis of the porosity test results (Fig. 5) and is also an important reason why the mechanical properties of the GO cement are higher than that of the control group. Since the pore structure of the GO cement is denser than that of the control group in the early stage of the reaction (16 h), the hydration products of the control cement are more likely to carbonate with ScCO2 to form CaCO3 in the subsequent synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction process, resulting in the relative content of CaCO3 in the control cement after 72 h being greater than that of GO.

It is worth noting that the content of Ca(OH)2 in the cement paste of the GO and the control group increases and then decreases, the main reason for this phenomenon is that in the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction of the cement paste, the hydration reaction dominates firstly, generating more Ca(OH)2, which increases the content of Ca(OH)2. With the extension of the reaction time, the hydration products began to participate in the carbonation reaction, and Ca(OH)2 was consumed to produce the carbonation product CaCO3. With the extension of the reaction time, Ca(OH)2 was consumed more and more, which led to the increase and then the decrease of Ca(OH)2 in the GO and control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR analysis of cement during hydration-carbonation synergistic reactions
ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the changes in the phase composition of the specimens after different hydration and carbonation reaction ages (4–72 h), FTIR tests were performed with the control cement and the GO cement in this work, and the resulting profiles are shown in Fig. 11. The vibrational peak at 3450 cm−1 in Fig. 11(a) and (b) originates from the O-H bond in H2O, the weak absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 is attributed to the bending band of O-H groups in water, and the other peak at 3600 cm−1 is related to the O-H bond of Ca(OH)2, which is always present during the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction [33]. The peak observed at 985 cm−1 is an asymmetric stretching vibration of the Si-O-T bond of C-S-H (where T stands for tetrahedral silicon or aluminium) [34,35]. During carbonization, the energy bands shift to higher wavenumbers as silicate aggregation increases [36]. Consequently, the onset of the carbonation reaction shifted this peak from 985 cm−1 in the early stages of the hydration-carbonation reaction (4–16 h) to 1043 cm−1 (72 h), indicating C-S-H decalcification and the formation of an amorphous silicate gel with a high degree of aggregation [37]. At this time, intense carbonation occurred within the cement of both the control and GO groups.

The strong peaks in the vibrational bands of GO and control cement around 1400 cm−1 correspond to symmetric stretching vibrations of [CO3]2- or asymmetric stretching of ions [38], and the peaks at 877 cm−1 and 712 cm−1 also correspond to [CO3]2- bending vibrations [39]. However, the [CO3]2- bending vibration corresponding to the peak at 877 cm−1 was much more pronounced in the early phase of the hydration-carbonation reaction (4–24 h) in GO cement, indicating that the content of [CO3]2- was increased in the early phase of the hydration-carbonation reaction in the GO samples compared to the control. It was also shown that the peak absorption bands corresponding to the calcite formed by cement carbonation were 1400 cm−1, 875 cm−1 and 713 cm−1, which is generally consistent with the experimental FTIR results in this work [40]. In addition, amorphous CaCO3 has been reported to have a vibrational absorption band at 1074 cm−1, while C-S-H also has a vibrational absorption band at 1083 cm−1 [38]. However, in the experimental results of this work, the corresponding peaks in these two bands could not be found, while absorption bands at 1108 cm−1 (GO) and 1100 cm−1 (control) were generated, which might be due to the interaction of part of the amorphous calcium carbonate with the C-S-H gel after the hydration-carbonation coupling reaction of the cement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-morphological analysis of cement during the hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 shows SEM images of control cement and GO cement after different ages of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction (4, 12, 24 and 72 h). Fig. 13(a) shows mainly pasty C-S-H gels and some granules. EDS elemental analysis of the surface of the granules (spot 1) shows carbon, indicating that these granular crystals are carbonation products (CaCO3 crystals) [11]. Fig. 12(b) shows the presence of numerous flower-like crystals with smooth-edged granular crystals inside the GO cement. EDS elemental analysis of the granular crystals sampled at the spot (spot A) revealed the presence of element C. It is speculated that the granular crystals are calcite, a carbonation product (consistent with the control cement).

It is noteworthy that there is a marked difference in the morphology of the hydration products in Fig. 12(a) and (b), i.e., a large number of flower-like crystals appear in the GO group (Fig. 12(b)), a morphological feature not present in the control group (Fig. 12(a)). The highly regular flower-like structure of GO cement after 4 h of synergistic reaction is mainly the hydration product C-S-H, which is consistent with the phenomenon found by Lu, which is attributed to the "carbon dot effect" of GO [41,42]. When the synergistic reaction lasted up to 12 h, the interior of the cement of the control group (Fig. 12(c)) was dominated by C-S-H gels, granular calcite and lamellar Ca(OH)2. In contrast, the GO cement (Fig. 12(d)) still shows a large number of flower-shaped crystals as well as densely distributed granular crystals around them. Compared with the synergistic reaction for 4 h (Fig. 12(b)), the flowery C-S-H arrangement on the GO cement surface appeared to be denser after 12 h of reaction (Fig. 12(d)). The EDS elemental analysis of the selected point B and its results show that there is a large amount of elemental C (19.27 wt%) in this flower-like structure, indicating that the formation of flower-like C-S-H after the early hydration-carbonation synergy reaction may induce the formation of acicular aragonite-type CaCO3 intertwined with the flower-like C-S-H during the subsequent synergy reaction. This phenomenon is closely related to the abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of GO. These functional groups effectively controlled the nucleation and growth direction of C-S-H and further promoted the growth of aragonite CaCO3 crystals [43].

When the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction continued for up to 24 and 72 h, the interior of the control cement was predominantly a C-S-H gel with granular calcite (Fig. 12(e) and (g)). In contrast, in the GO cement, the early-formed flower-like C-S-H structures were found to exhibit a continuous growth trend with a significant increase in grain size (Fig. 12(f) and (h)). EDS elemental analyzes at points C and D indicate that this needle-like structure is aragonite-type CaCO3 dispersed with flower-like C-S-H aggregates, resulting in a denser cement structure. This also explains why the pore structure of the control cement in Fig. 6 is not as dense as that of the GO cement (Fig. 7), which is closely related to the orderly growth of CaCO3 seed crystals induced by GO through the "carbon dot effect". It is worth noting that the growth process of C-S-H also has a crucial impact on the development of pore structure and mechanical properties of cement stone. Subsequently, the growth process of C-S-H will be characterized by 29Si spectral NMR and the influence of GO on the growth of C-S-H during the invasion of ScCO2 into the cement slurry-solid transition stage will be clarified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR analysis of the 29Si spectrum of cement during synergistic hydration-carbonation reactions
ce:section.ce:para: The 29Si spectral NMR curves of the control and GO cement after 4, 12, 24 and 72 h of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction are shown in Fig. 13. The chemical shifts of the different resonance peaks are denoted as Qn (n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) and the Qn peaks indicate the degree of polymerization of the [SiO4] units in the calcium silicate phase, where n is the number of oxygen bonds between the [SiO4] units [44,45]. The 29Si spectral NMR curves of the control and GO cements after 4, 12, 24 and 72 h of synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction are shown in Fig. 13. Q0 near −72.1 ppm represents unreacted isolated silicate tetrahedra, and the Q1 peaks Q2 peaks at −79.5 ppm and −85.2 ppm represent chain structures in the C-S-H gel (i.e., the end part of the chain and the middle part of the chain). The Q3 peak at about −102.8 ppm refers to an amorphous silica gel with a low degree of polymerization at the junction of the two C-S-H chains, while the Q4 peak at −116.3 ppm indicates a highly polymerized amorphous silica gel. The resulting profiles were deconvoluted using Origin 2022 software. The deconvolution results of the hydration-carbonation coupling reaction products of cement from control and GO groups are shown in Table 8.

The results in Fig. 13 show that the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction led to different changes in the C-S-H structure in the control and GO cement pastes. After the synergistic reaction was carried out for up to 4 h (Fig. 13(a) and (b)), the C-S-H structure in the control and GO cement consisted mainly of Q0, Q1 and Q2. The peak intensity of Q0 gradually decreased as the reaction progressed up to 72 h (Fig. 13(g) and (h)). The Q0 content of the control group in the deconvolution results in Table 8 decreased from 47.6 % to 6.40 % and the Q0 content in the GO group shrank from 35.1 % to 4.30 %. It can be seen from Table 8 that the deconvolution results of Q0 in the GO group were lower than those of the control cement at each age of the synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation. This indicates that the inclusion of GO led to an acceleration of the hydration reaction of the cement, resulting in a rapid decrease in the content of Q0 in the cement and the formation of more Q1 and Q2 through hydration. However, the relative contents of Q1 and Q2 peaked up to 12 h after the hydration-carbonation reaction. Q1 and Q2 were 27.6 % and 28.1 % in the control cement paste and 29.1 % and 39.4 % in the GO group, indicating that the incorporation of GO resulted in the formation of more C-S-H gels in the cement.

After the hydration-carbonation reaction was carried out for up to 24 h, the peak intensities of Q0, Q1 and Q2 in the control and GO cement (Fig. 13(e) and (f)) were significantly reduced and Q3 and Q4 were significantly increased. As shown in Table 8, the relative contents of Q3 and Q4 in the control group reached a peak value of 44.3 % and 21.3 %, respectively, after 24 h of reaction, while the relative contents of Q3 and Q4 in the GO cement were 15.5 % and 12.6 %, respectively. This indicates that more C-S-H gel was decalcified to amorphous silica gel in the control cement after 24 h of reaction, while the incorporation of GO slowed down the decalcification process of C-S-H. After 72 h of reaction, the relative contents of Q1 and Q2 in the GO group were 12.3 % and 27.9 %, respectively, significantly higher than in the control group (9.8 % and 18.6 %). The relative levels of Q3 and Q4 in the GO group also increased significantly compared to the levels after 24 h, which were 38.7 % and 19.1 %, respectively. This indicates that after 72 h of synergistic reaction, there were more C-S-H gels inside the GO cement, which produced less silica gel from the decalcification reaction than the control group.

The Q1/Q2 ratio is related to the degree of polymerization of C-S-H under early carbonation preservation, with lower Q1/Q2 ratios indicating a higher degree of polymerization of C-S-H [46]. Based on the unfolding results in Table 8, the average chain length (MCL) and degree of polymerization (PD) of C-S-H were calculated according to Eqs. (6) and (7) [47,48], and the results are shown in Fig. 14. On the other hand, the average chain length of GO cement C-S-H increased from 3.87 to 6.53 and the Q1/Q2 ratio decreased from 1.07 to 0.44. The reason for this phenomenon is the decrease of Ca/Si ratio of C-S-H and the acceleration of cement hydration due to the maintenance of carbonation [49]. The acceleration of cement hydration may also lead to an increase in the average chain length, which is consistent with other 29Si NMR measurements [50]. In addition, Fig. 14 shows that the average chain length and degree of polymerization of C-S-H of the control cement are smaller than those of the GO cement at each age of the hydration-carbonation coupling reaction. This indicates that the incorporation of GO resulted in a significant increase in the average chain length and degree of polymerization of C-S-H produced by the cement in the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction.

C-S-H is the main source of cement strength, and the greater its average chain length and the higher its degree of polymerization, the greater the strength of the cement. This is also an important reason for the superiority of both the mechanical properties (Fig. 4) and pore structure (Figs. 5 and 6) of GO cement over the control cement. The experimental 29Si NMR results show that the incorporation of GO not only accelerates the hydration reaction of cement to produce more C-S-H but also increases the MCL and PD of C-S-H.

Based on the experimental results in this paper, it is found that GO can optimize the microstructure of C-S-H and carbonation products of cement hydration during the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction, thereby reducing the porosity and increasing the compressive strength of cement under ScCO2 environment. The changes in internal hydration and carbonation reaction products during the synergistic hydration and carbonation reaction of cement are shown in Fig. 15.

As can be seen from Fig. 15, the mineral components (C3S, C2S) in the cement initially react with H2O to form Ca(OH)2 and a small amount of C-S-H (Q1 and Q2) after 4 h of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction process of the control cement slurry, at which time the polymerization degree of C-S-H is low and the average chain length is short. In addition, the hydration products Ca(OH)2, C-S-H and the incompletely hydrated mineral fractions (C3S and C2S) are carbonated with CO2 to form CaCO3 crystals. The relative content of Ca(OH)2 in the control cement peaked after 24 h of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction. This indicates that the hydration reaction of the control cement is essentially complete at this time and the carbonation reaction dominates in the subsequent synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction process. After the reaction was carried out for up to 72 h, the results of XRD and TG showed a continuous increase in the relative content of CaCO3. The results of 29Si spectroscopy NMR also showed a gradual weakening of the peaks of Q1 and Q2 and the beginning of the decalcification of C-S-H to form Q3 and Q4 (amorphous silica). The calculations in Fig. 14 showed that the degree of polymerization and the average chain length of C-S-H increased throughout the process of the coupled hydration-carbonation reaction (Fig. 4). which explains the increase in the compressive strength of the control cement (Fig. 4).

In addition, the microstructural changes of the GO cement paste during the synergistic reaction between hydration and carbonation were explained in Fig. 15. It can be seen from Fig. 15 that the incorporation of GO can significantly change the morphology of the hydration and carbonation products formed during the coupled hydration-carbonation reaction of the control cement, which in turn affects the macroscopic mechanical properties of the cement. The conclusion from the experimental results of this work, analyzing the effect of GO on the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction of cement, is mainly reflected in the following three aspects:

First, the incorporation of GO accelerates the hydration reaction of the cement, and the results of the physical analysis (XRD and TG) show that the peak of the relative content of the hydration products of the cement Ca(OH)2 in the GO group is at 12 h, while that of the control group is at 16 h. 29Si spectral NMR analyzes showed that GO cement had a lower relative content of internal incompletely hydrated Q0 than the control group and a significantly higher content of Q1 and Q2 at each age of the hydration-carbonation reaction.

Secondly, GO accelerated the early carbonation reaction of the cement paste and refined the porosity in the early structure of the cement. The results of XRD and TG analyzes showed that the relative content of CaCO3 crystals formed in the GO cement was greater than that in the control group at each reaction age. This indicates that the CaCO3 crystals formed can replenish the microporosity of the cement paste and reduce the porosity of the cement from 2.15 % to 1.22 % after 72 h of the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction (Table 5).

Third, some of the hydration products C-S-H and carbonation products (CaCO3) formed during the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction of cement are distributed in the form of flower clusters due to the "carbon dot effect" of GO, as shown in Fig. 12. The calculations of 29Si spectral NMR analysis (Fig. 13) showed that the degree of polymerization of C-S-H chain after 72 h was only 0.44, which was lower than that of the control group (0.52), and the average chain length of C-S-H chain was 6.53, which was significantly higher than that of the control group (5.79). It can be seen that when the C-S-H chains appear in a flower-like structure, their average chain length increases significantly, which increases the mechanical properties of the cement. In addition, the "carbon dot effect" of GO at the beginning of the carbonation reaction can lead to the formation of aragonite-type CaCO3 within the cement (Fig. 8), and the SEM images show that aragonite-type CaCO3 is present in the form of flower clusters (Fig. 12). The presence of flower-shaped CaCO3 fills the pores between the C-S-H gels and optimizes the microstructure of the cement. The significant increase in the degree of C-S-H polymerization and the average chain length, as well as the filling and refinement of the pores by the flower-shaped CaCO3, resulted in the porosity of the GO cement being only 0.93 % in the reaction up to 28 days, which was significantly lower than that of the control of 1.88 %. The reduction in cementitious porosity is also an important reason for the greater compressive strength of the GO cement compared to the control.

It is worth mentioning that ScCO2 is a highly diffusible liquid that can penetrate into the cement [51,52]. Since ScCO2 is an acidic gas, it will definitely change the internal structure of the cement when it reacts with the cement over a long period of time. Studies have shown that the long-term corrosive effect of ScCO2 can significantly enlarge the internal pores of cement, reduce the mechanical properties of cement, and pose a serious threat to the long-term safe operation of cement in ScCO2 gas-rich geologic environments [53,54]. The "carbon point effect" of GO in ordinary silicate cement leads to the formation of aragonite-type CaCO3 during the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction as well as the formation of C-S-H in flower clusters. Aragonite-type CaCO3 is also arranged in the form of flower clusters during the synergistic reaction. Flower clusters of CaCO3 and C-S-H can refine the porosity and microstructure of liquid cement.

At the same time, GO can also improve the microstructure of cement by increasing the degree of polymerization and average chain length of C-S-H cement, which in turn improves the compressive strength of cement. The densification of the internal pore structure of GO cement will slow down the infiltration and corrosion of ScCO2 on cement and improve the reliability of ordinary silicate cement for long-term use in a geological environment with high ScCO2 content. According to the results of previous studies, it is not only possible to incorporate materials such as latex, rubber and fibres to increase the density of cement. The experimental results in this paper show that it is also possible to try to change the structure of the hydration-carbonation reaction products of cementite to increase the density of cement and further enhance the corrosion resistance of cementite in the CCUS environment. Therefore, the incorporation of GO provides new ideas for the design of CCUS cementing systems.

Under the ScCO2 environment, the mechanical properties and porosity of cement were significantly improved after the addition of GO. The presence of GO not only accelerates the early carbonation reaction, but also effectively controls the nucleation and growth direction of C-S-H and CaCO3 crystals to produce flower cluster-like reaction products. In the supercritical CO2 gas environment, the incorporation of GO will optimize the microstructure of the cement in the liquid-to-solid transition phase. This serves as a guide for the development of cement systems with excellent mechanical properties and dense pores suitable for ScCO2 environments. The conclusions of the study are summarized below:
(1)
By inducing the crystallization and orderly growth of CaCO3 and C-S-H, GO can refine the pore structure of CCUS cementing cement and enhance its compressive strength. The growth rate of compressive strength of GO cement reached 2.90 MPa/d in 1–3 days, which was higher than that of the control group (2.55 MPa/d), and the compressive strength and growth rate of GO cement were higher than those of the control group at each stage during the subsequent reaction period. The porosity of GO cement after the synergistic hydration-carbonation reaction was 1.22 % and 0.93 % after 3 and 28 days, which were significantly lower than the 2.15 % and 1.88 % of the control group, respectively;
(2)
GO can increase the synergistic reaction rate between the hydration and carbonation of cement and influence the relative content of hydration and carbonation reaction products. The results of physical phase analysis showed that the presence of GO enriched the crystalline form of carbonation product CaCO3 in cement and aragonite-type CaCO3 appeared. The hydration product Ca(OH)2 in the GO cement reached the peak of the hydration-carbonation coupling reaction at 12 h, significantly earlier than in the control group at 16 h.
(3)
The mechanism of GO-induced crystallization led to the orderly growth of C-S-H seed crystals in the early hydration-carbonation synergistic reaction, and the polymerization degree and average chain length were significantly improved. The 29Si NMR results show that the polymerization degree and average chain length of GO cement are higher than those of the control at each reaction age. After 72 h, the degree of polymerization of the C-S-H chain in the control group was only 0.44, which was lower than that in the GO group (0.52). The average chain length of C-S-H chain in the GO group was 6.53, which was significantly higher than that in the control group (5.79).
(4)
The "carbon dot effect" of GO induces the formation of flower-clustered CaCO3 and C-S-H seed crystals in CCUS cementing during the liquid-solid transition stage. With the extension of the hydration-carbonization synergistic reaction time, the flower clusters of CaCO3 and C-S-H seed crystals grew in an orderly manner. These orderly growth flower cluster crystals can be filled in the center of the C-S-H chain network, which can refine the pores of cement, improve its microstructure, and improve its mechanical properties.
